note: Module 2 — the demand side. Why anyone would want capacity or service in SEA. This is the largest decision driver and the least documented in public data; treat as directional, not precise. Quality tags apply.

structural_drivers.what_it_is: Since US export controls tightened (2022 onward), customers and OSATs have diversified assembly/test and some mature-node capacity out of China and, increasingly, out of single-Taiwan dependence. SEA is the primary landing zone for back-end diversification.
structural_drivers.relevance_to_specialty_foundry: Creates pull for geographically diversified mature/specialty capacity and for non-China back-end. It favors SEA back-end/OSAT and raises the option value of a non-Taiwan mature-node footprint — but the pull lands mostly on packaging/test, not front-end.

structural_drivers.what_it_is: US–China tariffs and potential semiconductor-specific tariffs push final-assembly and country-of-origin decisions toward SEA to preserve market access.
structural_drivers.relevance_to_specialty_foundry: Strengthens SEA assembly/test demand and could pull some substrate/packaging value; effect on front-end wafer siting is indirect. Policy is in flux — directional only.

structural_drivers.what_it_is: Automotive, industrial and MCU customers increasingly require a non-Taiwan source for mature/specialty nodes.
structural_drivers.relevance_to_specialty_foundry: Directly relevant to a specialty foundry's diversification thesis — but the credible non-Taiwan front-end answer in the region is Singapore (and peers building there), not the other five countries.

structural_drivers.what_it_is: SEA hosts large downstream demand: automotive & power (Thailand), electronics/EMS assembly (Vietnam, Malaysia, Philippines), and a fast-growing data-center cluster (Johor/Singapore).
structural_drivers.relevance_to_specialty_foundry: Downstream gravity pulls mature/specialty silicon (PMIC, power, RF, display-driver, MCU) into the region's supply chains and creates back-end and support-engineering adjacency — the most realistic SEA demand channel for a specialty foundry.

end_market_gravity.end_markets: Broad E&E, advanced packaging/substrates, and a large Johor data-center build-out adjacent to Singapore.
end_market_gravity.foundry_relevant_pull: Back-end and advanced-packaging adjacency is the strongest of the five; DC cluster adds power/analog demand context.

end_market_gravity.end_markets: Large FDI electronics/EMS base (Samsung and suppliers); rising IC-design/R&D.
end_market_gravity.foundry_relevant_pull: Demand is assembly- and design-driven; front-end pull is low. Relevant to support/FAE and packaging adjacency.

end_market_gravity.end_markets: Automotive, power electronics, HDD, and broad electronics manufacturing; EEC corridor.
end_market_gravity.foundry_relevant_pull: Mature-node and power/automotive demand is the clearest specialty channel — but the EV transition is reshaping the automotive base, so read as evolving.

end_market_gravity.end_markets: EV and local-content-driven electronics demand emerging; large domestic market.
end_market_gravity.foundry_relevant_pull: Thin and policy-driven; demand is potential, not yet a supply-chain reality for specialty silicon.

end_market_gravity.end_markets: ATP and electronics exports (world's 9th largest chip exporter, OECD); EMS base.
end_market_gravity.foundry_relevant_pull: Back-end/ATP demand and English-language support; minimal design pull. Relevant to support operations, not front-end.
